title: Gateway Goes Gigabit, Wireless
author: Kirk L. Kroeker, TechNewsWorld
abstract: TechNewsWorld article (Apr 6 2004, Kirk L. Kroeker) on Gateway's entry into enterprise networking with the 7200/7400/7600 Series Layer 2 managed and unmanaged network switches (based on Broadcom switch silicon) and the Gateway 7000 Series Wi-Fi access points (802.11g / 802.11a+g, Intel IPX422 processor, PoE, RADIUS, VLAN). Launched 'in the aftermath of a major announcement about shutting all its retail stores' — Gateway exiting brick-and-mortar after eMachines acquisition. Aberdeen EVP and chief research officer Peter Kastner provides the strategic framing: 'In both their price and functionality, Gateway is taking a disruptive approach with these two new wireless [access points]. Many wireless vendors are ignoring the pleas of small- and medium-sized businesses, who desire enterprise security and much more simplicity in setup. SMBs clearly stand to benefit from these new Gateway [access points].' IDC's Maximilian Flisi: managed switches priced competitively and may be a lead purchase driver.
importance_rationale: Documents Gateway's post-retail pivot toward enterprise networking (alongside servers and storage) — a pivot that ultimately failed when Acer acquired Gateway in 2007 and retired the enterprise line. Kastner's SMB-disruption framing correctly identified underserved market that Ubiquiti (founded 2005), Meraki (2006), and Aruba Instant (2012) later exploited.
relevance_rationale: SMB-networking underservice thesis Kastner articulated has driven a 20-year market: Ubiquiti IPO 2011, Cisco Meraki acquisition $1.2B 2012, HPE Aruba Instant, Juniper Mist, and cloud-managed network-as-a-service. The specific Gateway products are historical but the market gap they targeted became a major industry category.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's SMB-disruption-opportunity call was directionally correct: SMB networking became a major growth category — but Gateway itself failed to capture it. Acer acquired Gateway for $710M in Oct 2007 and retired the enterprise networking line; Ubiquiti, Meraki, and later Aruba Instant / HPE captured the SMB cloud-managed-Wi-Fi market Kastner identified.
